skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.1-1.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Basic Economic Principles for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.1-1.6). Students should understand the foundational principles that underpin microeconomic analysis. Key concepts: scarcity forces choices, and every choice has an opportunity cost (the value of the next best alternative); rational decision-making involves marginal analysis (comparing marginal benefit to marginal cost); the production possibilities curve (PPC) illustrates trade-offs, efficiency (points on the curve), inefficiency (points inside), and impossibility (points outside); comparative advantage and specialization explain why trade is mutually beneficial; factors of production (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship) are scarce resources. Students should construct and interpret PPC graphs, calculate opportunity costs from data tables, determine comparative advantage, and apply marginal analysis to decision-making. The concepts of trade-offs and marginal thinking are foundational for all subsequent microeconomic topics. Vocabulary: scarcity, opportunity cost, marginal analysis, PPC, efficiency, comparative advantage, specialization, trade-off, marginal benefit, marginal cost.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand (Topic 2.1-2.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Supply and Demand for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand, Topic 2.1-2.6). Students should understand how prices and quantities are determined in competitive markets. Key concepts: the law of demand (inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded); demand shifters (income, preferences, prices of related goods, expectations, number of buyers); the law of supply (positive relationship between price and quantity supplied); supply shifters (input prices, technology, expectations, number of sellers, government regulation); market equilibrium occurs where supply and demand intersect; surpluses (excess supply above equilibrium) drive prices down; shortages (excess demand below equilibrium) drive prices up; government interventions include price ceilings (below equilibrium, causing shortages) and price floors (above equilibrium, causing surpluses). Students should graph supply and demand, shift curves for various scenarios, and determine new equilibrium outcomes. Vocabulary: demand, supply, equilibrium, surplus, shortage, price ceiling, price floor, demand shifter, supply shifter, quantity demanded, quantity supplied.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand (Topic 2.3-2.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Elasticity for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand, Topic 2.3-2.5). Students should understand how responsive buyers and sellers are to price changes. Key concepts: price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price; elastic demand (PED > 1) means quantity is highly responsive to price; inelastic demand (PED < 1) means quantity is less responsive; unit elastic (PED = 1); determinants of elasticity (availability of substitutes, necessity vs. luxury, proportion of income, time); the relationship between elasticity and total revenue (elastic: lower price increases revenue; inelastic: lower price decreases revenue); cross-price elasticity (positive for substitutes, negative for complements); income elasticity (positive for normal goods, negative for inferior goods); price elasticity of supply. Students should calculate elasticity using the midpoint method and analyze its implications for pricing and revenue. Vocabulary: elasticity, elastic, inelastic, unit elastic, total revenue test, cross-price elasticity, income elasticity, midpoint method, substitutes, complements.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.5-1.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Consumer Choice and Utility for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.5-1.6). Students should understand how consumers make decisions to maximize their satisfaction. Key concepts: utility is a measure of satisfaction or happiness derived from consuming a good; total utility increases with consumption but at a decreasing rate; marginal utility is the additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit; the law of diminishing marginal utility states that each additional unit provides less additional satisfaction; the utility-maximizing rule states that consumers maximize utility when the marginal utility per dollar spent is equal across all goods (MUa/Pa = MUb/Pb); this rule explains why demand curves slope downward (as price increases, MU per dollar decreases, so consumers buy less). Students should apply the utility-maximizing rule to determine optimal consumption bundles and understand how it connects to the demand curve. Vocabulary: utility, marginal utility, total utility, diminishing marginal utility, utility maximization, marginal utility per dollar, consumer choice, satisfaction, optimal.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model (Topic 3.1-3.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Production and Costs for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model, Topic 3.1-3.3). Students should understand how firms produce goods and the cost structures they face. Key concepts: the short run has at least one fixed input; the long run has all variable inputs; the production function relates inputs to outputs; marginal product (MP) is the additional output from one more unit of input; diminishing marginal returns occur when MP begins to decline; total cost = fixed cost + variable cost; average total cost (ATC) = TC/Q; average variable cost (AVC) = VC/Q; marginal cost (MC) = change in TC / change in Q; the MC curve intersects ATC and AVC at their minimum points; economies of scale (decreasing LRATC), diseconomies of scale (increasing LRATC), and constant returns to scale in the long run. Students should calculate and graph all cost curves and understand the relationships between them. Vocabulary: fixed cost, variable cost, total cost, marginal cost, average total cost, marginal product, diminishing returns, economies of scale, short run, long run.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model (Topic 3.4-3.7)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Perfect Competition for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 3: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model, Topic 3.4-3.7). Students should understand the model of a perfectly competitive market. Key concepts: characteristics include many small firms, identical products, perfect information, and free entry/exit; firms are price takers (cannot influence market price); the demand curve facing an individual firm is perfectly elastic (horizontal) at the market price; profit-maximizing output is where MR = MC (and MR = P in perfect competition); economic profit = (P - ATC) x Q; in the short run, firms produce if P >= AVC (shutdown point) even if experiencing losses; in the long run, free entry and exit drive economic profit to zero (P = ATC at the minimum); the long-run supply curve depends on whether the industry has constant, increasing, or decreasing costs. Students should graph firm-level and market-level diagrams side by side and determine profit/loss outcomes. Vocabulary: perfect competition, price taker, MR = MC, economic profit, normal profit, shutdown point, break-even, long-run equilibrium, free entry, allocative efficiency.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.1-4.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Monopoly for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.1-4.3). Students should understand how a single-seller market operates differently from perfect competition. Key concepts: a monopolist is the sole seller with no close substitutes; sources of monopoly power include barriers to entry (legal barriers, patents, control of resources, economies of scale/natural monopoly); the monopolist faces the market demand curve (downward-sloping); marginal revenue is less than price because the firm must lower price on all units to sell additional units; profit maximization at MR = MC; the monopolist charges a price above MC, creating a deadweight loss (allocative inefficiency); monopolists earn economic profit in both the short run and long run (barriers prevent entry); natural monopoly occurs when one firm can serve the entire market at lower cost than multiple firms; government regulation of monopoly (price regulation, antitrust). Students should graph monopoly and identify profit, deadweight loss, and consumer/producer surplus. Vocabulary: monopoly, barriers to entry, price maker, deadweight loss, natural monopoly, antitrust, price discrimination, economic profit, market power.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.4-4.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Oligopoly for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.4-4.5). Students should understand markets dominated by a few large firms whose decisions are interdependent. Key concepts: oligopoly features few dominant firms, significant barriers to entry, and strategic interdependence (each firm's decisions affect and are affected by rivals' decisions); products may be identical (steel) or differentiated (automobiles); game theory provides the framework for analyzing strategic behavior; the prisoners' dilemma illustrates why firms may not cooperate even when cooperation would benefit both; collusion (firms agreeing to restrict output and raise prices) is illegal but tempting; cartels (like OPEC) are formal collusion agreements; the kinked demand curve model explains price rigidity; firms compete through non-price competition (advertising, quality, innovation) rather than price wars. Students should analyze payoff matrices, identify dominant strategies and Nash equilibrium, and understand why cartels tend to break down. Vocabulary: oligopoly, interdependence, game theory, prisoners' dilemma, collusion, cartel, Nash equilibrium, dominant strategy, kinked demand, payoff matrix.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Monopolistic Competition for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.6). Students should understand the market structure that combines elements of monopoly and perfect competition. Key concepts: monopolistic competition features many firms, differentiated products (real or perceived differences through branding, quality, location), relatively easy entry and exit, and some degree of market power; because products are differentiated, each firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve (like monopoly) but with many substitutes (making demand relatively elastic); short-run profit maximization at MR = MC (can earn economic profit or loss); in the long run, entry and exit drive economic profit to zero (P = ATC), but P > MC (not allocatively efficient) and firms produce below minimum ATC (excess capacity). Students should graph short-run and long-run equilibrium and compare monopolistic competition to both perfect competition and monopoly. Vocabulary: monopolistic competition, product differentiation, excess capacity, branding, short-run profit, long-run zero profit, non-price competition, advertising, elastic demand.

skills.ref: AP Micro Units 3-4: Market Structures Comparison
skills.prompt: This topic covers Market Structures for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Units 3-4: Market Structures Comparison). Students should understand how to compare and contrast the four market structures. Key concepts: the four structures differ along key dimensions: number of firms (many to one), type of product (identical to unique), barriers to entry (none to insurmountable), price-making ability (none to complete), long-run economic profit (zero to positive), and efficiency (both allocative and productive). Perfect competition is the efficiency benchmark (P = MC, P = minimum ATC in long run); monopoly is the least efficient (P > MC, deadweight loss); monopolistic competition has long-run zero profit but excess capacity; oligopoly's outcomes depend on the degree of competition or collusion. Students should be able to identify real-world examples of each structure, compare their graphical representations side by side, and evaluate the welfare implications (consumer surplus, producer surplus, deadweight loss) of each. Vocabulary: market structure, perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, monopolistic competition, efficiency, deadweight loss, barriers to entry, market power, welfare.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets (Topic 5.1-5.4)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Factor Markets for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets, Topic 5.1-5.4). Students should understand how the markets for resources (inputs) work, particularly the labor market. Key concepts: the demand for factors of production is a derived demand (derived from the demand for the goods they produce); marginal revenue product (MRP = MR x MP) measures the additional revenue from hiring one more unit of a factor; the profit-maximizing hiring rule is MRP = factor price (MRP = wage for labor); the MRP curve is the firm's demand curve for labor; the labor supply curve shows the relationship between the wage and the quantity of labor supplied; market equilibrium wage is where labor supply equals labor demand; monopsony (single buyer of labor) pays lower wages and hires fewer workers than a competitive labor market. Students should graph factor markets, determine profit-maximizing employment levels, and analyze monopsony and minimum wage effects. Vocabulary: factor market, derived demand, marginal revenue product, MRP, wage, labor market, monopsony, human capital, marginal product, hiring rule.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Market Failure for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.1). Students should understand when and why free markets fail to achieve allocatively efficient outcomes. Key concepts: market failure occurs when the free market does not produce the socially optimal quantity of a good; sources of market failure include externalities (costs or benefits imposed on third parties), public goods (non-excludable, non-rivalrous goods that the market underproduces), market power (monopoly/oligopoly producing too little at too high a price), asymmetric information (one party has more information than another, leading to adverse selection and moral hazard), and income inequality. When market failure occurs, government intervention may improve outcomes through taxes, subsidies, regulations, or direct provision. Students should identify the type of market failure present in a given scenario and evaluate potential remedies. Vocabulary: market failure, externality, public good, market power, asymmetric information, adverse selection, moral hazard, efficiency, deadweight loss, government intervention.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.2-6.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Externalities for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.2-6.3). Students should understand how costs or benefits that affect third parties lead to market inefficiency. Key concepts: negative externalities (pollution, noise, congestion) impose costs on others not involved in the transaction; the market overproduces goods with negative externalities because the social cost exceeds the private cost; positive externalities (education, vaccinations, research) provide benefits to others beyond the buyer and seller; the market underproduces goods with positive externalities because the social benefit exceeds the private benefit. Solutions: for negative externalities, use per-unit taxes (Pigouvian taxes), regulations, cap-and-trade systems, or property rights assignment (Coase Theorem); for positive externalities, use subsidies, government provision, or mandates. Students should graph externalities showing the divergence between private and social costs/benefits and identify the socially optimal quantity. Vocabulary: externality, negative externality, positive externality, social cost, private cost, social benefit, Pigouvian tax, subsidy, Coase Theorem, deadweight loss, optimal quantity.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.4)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Public Goods for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.4). Students should understand why certain goods cannot be efficiently provided by private markets. Key concepts: public goods are non-excludable (cannot prevent non-payers from consuming) and non-rivalrous (one person's consumption does not reduce availability to others); examples include national defense, street lighting, clean air, and public parks; the free-rider problem means individuals can benefit without paying, so private firms cannot profitably provide public goods (they are underproduced or not produced at all by markets); government provision funded by taxation is the typical solution; common resources are rivalrous but non-excludable (fishing grounds, clean water), leading to the tragedy of the commons (overuse); private goods are both excludable and rivalrous. Students should classify goods into the four categories (public, private, common resources, club goods) and explain why market failure occurs for each type. Vocabulary: public good, non-excludable, non-rivalrous, free rider, tragedy of the commons, common resource, club good, private good, government provision, market failure.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Government Intervention for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.5). Students should understand the tools governments use to correct market failures and their trade-offs. Key concepts: price controls (price ceilings cause shortages, price floors cause surpluses); per-unit taxes reduce quantity, create deadweight loss, and the tax burden is shared based on elasticity (more inelastic side bears more burden); per-unit subsidies increase quantity and can correct positive externalities; antitrust laws prevent monopolistic behavior; regulations set standards for safety, environment, and quality; government provision of public goods; redistribution through taxes and transfer payments. Students should analyze the effects of government intervention using supply and demand graphs, identify unintended consequences (black markets from price ceilings, surplus from price floors), evaluate whether the intervention achieves its goal, and calculate tax incidence based on relative elasticities of supply and demand. Vocabulary: price control, tax incidence, per-unit tax, subsidy, regulation, antitrust, deadweight loss, tax burden, elasticity, unintended consequence.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets (Topic 5.1-5.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Labor Markets for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 5: Factor Markets, Topic 5.1-5.3). Students should understand how wages and employment levels are determined in different labor market structures. Key concepts: in a perfectly competitive labor market, firms are wage takers and hire until MRP = wage; the market wage is determined by the intersection of labor supply and labor demand; factors affecting labor demand include productivity, price of the output, and technology; factors affecting labor supply include population, immigration, education, and alternative opportunities; wage differentials arise from human capital differences, compensating differentials (higher pay for unpleasant or dangerous work), discrimination, and union power; minimum wage above the equilibrium creates unemployment but raises income for employed workers; monopsony (single employer) pays below the competitive wage and hires fewer workers. Students should graph competitive and monopsony labor markets and analyze policy effects. Vocabulary: labor market, wage rate, MRP, labor demand, labor supply, human capital, compensating differential, minimum wage, monopsony, wage differential, union.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government (Topic 6.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Income Distribution for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 6: Market Failure and Role of Government, Topic 6.6). Students should understand how income is distributed in market economies and government policies that address inequality. Key concepts: the Lorenz curve graphically represents income distribution (the further from the 45-degree line of equality, the greater the inequality); the Gini coefficient measures inequality (0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality); sources of income inequality include differences in human capital, inheritance, discrimination, market power, and globalization; poverty measures (poverty line, poverty rate); government redistribution tools include progressive taxation (higher earners pay higher rates), transfer payments (welfare, Social Security, Medicaid, food stamps), and in-kind benefits; the equity-efficiency trade-off (redistribution may reduce incentives to work and invest). Students should interpret Lorenz curves, discuss the trade-off between equity and efficiency, and evaluate redistribution policies. Vocabulary: income distribution, Lorenz curve, Gini coefficient, inequality, poverty, progressive taxation, transfer payments, redistribution, equity, efficiency trade-off.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition (Topic 4.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Game Theory for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 4: Imperfect Competition, Topic 4.5). Students should understand how strategic decision-making is modeled using game theory. Key concepts: game theory analyzes situations where the outcome depends on the choices of multiple decision-makers (players); a payoff matrix shows the outcomes for each combination of strategies; a dominant strategy is the best choice regardless of what the other player does; Nash equilibrium occurs when neither player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing strategy; the prisoners' dilemma demonstrates why rational self-interest can lead to collectively suboptimal outcomes; in the classic prisoners' dilemma, both players defect even though mutual cooperation would be better for both; repeated games can sustain cooperation through strategies like tit-for-tat. Students should read and analyze payoff matrices, identify dominant strategies and Nash equilibria, and apply game theory to real-world oligopoly decisions (pricing, advertising, output). Vocabulary: game theory, payoff matrix, dominant strategy, Nash equilibrium, prisoners' dilemma, cooperation, defection, strategic behavior, tit-for-tat, interdependence.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Behavioral Economics for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.6). Students should understand how real human decision-making deviates from the rational actor model assumed in traditional economics. Key concepts: traditional economics assumes rational, self-interested decision-makers with perfect information; behavioral economics (drawing on psychology) shows that people systematically deviate from rationality; bounded rationality means people make satisfactory rather than optimal decisions due to cognitive limitations; cognitive biases include loss aversion (losses feel worse than equivalent gains feel good), anchoring (over-relying on the first piece of information), status quo bias (preference for the current state), and present bias (overvaluing immediate rewards vs. future benefits); nudges are subtle policy interventions that guide behavior without restricting choice (opt-out vs. opt-in retirement savings). Students should identify how biases affect economic decisions and evaluate whether nudges can improve outcomes. Vocabulary: behavioral economics, bounded rationality, cognitive bias, loss aversion, anchoring, nudge, default option, present bias, heuristic, rational actor.

skills.ref: AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand (Topic 2.8-2.9)
skills.prompt: This topic covers International Trade and Microeconomics for AP Microeconomics students (AP Micro Unit 2: Supply and Demand, Topic 2.8-2.9). Students should understand how international trade affects domestic markets, prices, and welfare. Key concepts: trade is based on comparative advantage (producing where opportunity cost is lowest); when a country opens to trade at the world price, if the world price is below the domestic price, the country imports (consumers benefit, domestic producers lose); if the world price is above the domestic price, the country exports (domestic producers benefit, consumers pay more); tariffs raise the domestic price of imports, benefiting domestic producers and the government but harming consumers and creating deadweight loss; quotas restrict the quantity of imports, raising domestic prices similarly to tariffs but without government revenue; free trade maximizes total surplus but creates winners and losers. Students should graph the effects of trade, tariffs, and quotas on consumer surplus, producer surplus, government revenue, and deadweight loss. Vocabulary: comparative advantage, world price, domestic price, imports, exports, tariff, quota, consumer surplus, producer surplus, deadweight loss, free trade, protectionism.
